revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11412646]]
Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11412646]]
Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato state]] on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Paekche|Paekche]].  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, [[:en:Ōmi Province|omi]] of Upper [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi]], was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

==See also==
*[[:en:List of Japanese battles|List of Japanese battles]]
*[[:en:Military history of Japan|Military history of Japan]]

==Notes==
{{Reflist|group="nb"}}

==References==
{{Reflist|32em}}

===Bibliography===
*{{cite book|last1=Brinkley|first1=FRank|author-link=Francis Brinkley|title=A history of the Japanese people: From the earliest times to the end of the Meiji era|url=https://archive.org/details/ahistoryjapanes01kikugoog|year=1915|publisher=[[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.|Encyclopædia Britannica Co]]}}

{{Japanese coups |state=expanded}}

{{Kibi clan}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412646]]
Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato state]] on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Paekche|Paekche]].  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, [[:en:Ōmi Province|omi]] of Upper [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi]], was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato state]] on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Paekche|Paekche]].  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, [[:en:Ōmi Province|omi]] of Upper [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi]], was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

==Outline==
When [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Paekche|Paekche]].  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, [[:en:Ōmi Province|omi]] of Upper [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi]], was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

==See also==
*[[:en:List of Japanese battles|List of Japanese battles]]
*[[:en:Military history of Japan|Military history of Japan]]

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial! What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}
In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" /> Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" /> {{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}
However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}} Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}} After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412646]] Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412646]] Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}} {{Disputed|date=February 2020}} The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato state]] on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.
==Outline== When [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Paekche|Paekche]]. However relations with the neighboring kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}
In 463, Tasa, [[:en:Ōmi Province|omi]] of Upper [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi]], was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka: {{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial! What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}
In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" /> Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" /> {{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}
However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}} Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}} After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Japanese battles|List of Japanese battles]] *[[:en:Military history of Japan|Military history of Japan]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|group="nb"}}
==References== {{Reflist|32em}}
===Bibliography=== *{{cite book|last1=Brinkley|first1=FRank|author-link=Francis Brinkley|title=A history of the Japanese people: From the earliest times to the end of the Meiji era|url=https://archive.org/details/ahistoryjapanes01kikugoog|year=1915|publisher=[[Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.|Encyclopædia Britannica Co]]}}

The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] had moved him to the Japanese post at [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's [[:en:Protohistory|proto-historic]] period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

However relations with the neighboring kingdom of [[:en:Silla|Silla]] had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial! What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}
In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" /> Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" /> {{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}
However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}} Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}} After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the [[:en:Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion]].{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Paekche|en|Paekche|WD=Q28428|lt=Paekche}}.  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=omi}} of Upper {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi}}, was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the {{ill|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|en|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|WD=Q18027474|lt=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion}}.{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the {{ill|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|en|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|WD=Q18027474|lt=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion}}.{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]]

Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11412646]]
Wikidata name: Kibi Clan Rebellion
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Paekche|en|Paekche|WD=Q28428|lt=Paekche}}.  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=omi}} of Upper {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi}}, was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the {{ill|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|en|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|WD=Q18027474|lt=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion}}.{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kibi Clan Rebellion]][[fr:Rébellion du clan Kibi]][[ja:吉備氏の乱]][[ko:기비씨의 난]][[ru:Мятеж рода Киби]][[zh:吉備氏之亂]]


{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Paekche|en|Paekche|WD=Q28428|lt=Paekche}}.  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=omi}} of Upper {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi}}, was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the {{ill|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|en|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|WD=Q18027474|lt=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion}}.{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

==See also==
*{{ill|List of Japanese battles|en|List of Japanese battles|WD=Q839176|lt=List of Japanese battles}}
*{{ill|Military history of Japan|en|Military history of Japan|WD=Q2236203|lt=Military history of Japan}}

In 463, Tasa, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=omi|1=Ōmi Province}} of Upper {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi|1=Kingdom of Kibi}}, was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the {{ill|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|en|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|WD=Q18027474|lt=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|1=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion}}.{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>

{{Short description|463 revolt against the Yamato state}}
{{Disputed|date=February 2020}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kibi Clan Rebellion'''|吉備氏の乱|kibishi no ran}} from 463 was a revolt against the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} on the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]], involving two brothers from the [[Kibi clan]]: Tasa and Oto. The revolt was triggered when Tasa learned that the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} had moved him to the Japanese post at {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} on the Korean Peninsula in order to seize his beautiful wife.{{Dubious|date=November 2024}} The incident falls into Japan's {{ill|Protohistory|en|Protohistory|WD=Q695827|lt=proto-historic|1=Protohistory}} period and is recounted in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''.

==Outline==
When {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} ascended the throne in 456, Japan was on friendly terms with the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Paekche|en|Paekche|WD=Q28428|lt=Paekche|1=Paekche}}.  However relations with the neighboring kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} had been strained after the harsh treatment of their convoy in 453.<ref group="nb">According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], members of the convoy had been imprisoned and questioned being suspected to have had dealing with the female attendants (''uneme'') after referring to [[Mount Unebe]] by the (incorrect) name uneme.</ref><ref name = "brinkley-p114">{{Harvnb|Brinkley|1915|p=114}}</ref> Angered by this incident, Silla had reduced the number of ships between the countries and articles sent as tribute.<ref name= "nihongi">{{cite book |last= Aston |first= William G. |year= 1896 |title= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 | url = https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html}}</ref> Since 456, Silla had not sent the customary presents to the Japanese Emperor.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}

In 463, Tasa, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=omi|1=Ōmi Province}} of Upper {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi|1=Kingdom of Kibi}}, was at the Imperial Palace and speaking to his friends, praised his wife, Waka:
{{Quote |text =Of all the beautiful women in the Empire, there is none to compare with my wife. How blooming! How gentle! How graced with various charms! How radiant! How genial!  What perfection in every feature! She uses not flower of lead: she adds not oil of orchids. Through the wide ages her equals are but few: in the present day she stands alone and peerless.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

In order to possess Waka, Emperor Yūryaku had Tasa moved out of the way to the distant post of governor of Mimana and made Waka-hime one of his concubines.<ref name= "brinkley-p114"/><ref name= "nihongi"/>{{Sfn |Brinkley | 1915 | p = 112}}<ref name = "kamstra">{{cite book |last= Kamstra |first= Jacques H. |year= 1967 |title= Encounter Or Syncretism: The Initial Growth of Japanese Buddhism |publisher= Brill Archive|page= 255}}</ref> When Tasa learned that he had been robbed of his wife, he started a revolt looking for help from Silla.<ref name="kamstra"/> Yūryaku, known for his cruelty, ordered Tasa's son, Oto, to lead an army against his father.<ref name="brinkley-p114" /> Oto was joined by a group of men who wanted to secure skilled artisans from Paekche.<ref name = "nihongi" />  Oto had settled for several months in Paekche (without taking any action against Tasa or Silla) when he received a message from his father suggesting he hold Mimana and Paekche and break off communication with Japan:<ref name = "brinkley-p114" />
{{Quote |text=Do thou, my son, come over and betake thyself to Pekche, and prevent it from communicating with Japan, while I will repair to and hold Imna, and will also hold no communication with Japan.<ref name="nihongi" />}}

However Oto's wife, Kusu, was a very patriotic woman who foiled Tasa's plot by killing her husband.<ref name="brinkley-p114"/><ref name="nihongi"/> Over several years, Yūryaku sent four expeditions to Korea, but could not recover his hold on the peninsula.{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  Brinkley asserts that Japan lost its standing in Korea "because of Yūryaku's illicit passion for one of his subjects."{{Sfn | Brinkley | 1915 | pp = 114, 121}}  After the death of Yūryaku on the 7th day of the 8th month, 479, Prince Hoshikawa, encouraged by his mother, the "robbed" consort Waka-hime, claimed the throne against the designated crown prince, [[:en:Prince Shiraka|Shiraka]], which led to the {{ill|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|en|Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|WD=Q18027474|lt=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion|1=Prince Hoshikawa Rebellion}}.{{Sfn |Brinkley|1915|p = 117}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Ebersole |first=Gary L. |year=1992 |title=Ritual Poetry and the Politics of Death in Early Japan |publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] |isbn = 978-0-691-01929-1 |pages = 115–17}}</ref>
